varieties.name: NOLI
varieties.traits: Medium-sized round fruit, smooth to slightly bumpy skin, light green when mature
varieties.flesh_fruit: Creamy white, firm texture, excellent eating quality

varieties.name: MA'AFALA
varieties.traits: Dwarf variety, compact growth (starts fruiting at 3m height). Small to medium fruit (0.8-1.6 kg), round shape, smooth green skin
varieties.flesh_fruit: Creamy white turning yellow when cooked, light fluffy texture, excellent for mashing and patties. Highest protein content (6%) among varieties
varieties.yield_notes: Very high fruit numbers, prolific bearer
varieties.availability: Available through tissue culture propagation, specialty tropical nurseries in Queensland

varieties.name: SAMOAN SEEDLESS
varieties.origin: Samoa, brought to North Queensland early 1900s
varieties.traits: Large fruit, round to oval, green skin ripening yellow-green
varieties.flesh_fruit: White to cream, dense starchy texture

varieties.name: FIJI SEEDED (ULU FITI)
varieties.traits: Medium to large fruit, contains multiple edible seeds, green skin
varieties.flesh_fruit: Tender, moist, sweet when ripe. Seeds have chestnut-like flavor when cooked

varieties.name: HAWAIIAN 'ULU MAOLI
varieties.traits: Medium fruit (1.6 kg average), round, firm texture, green skin
varieties.flesh_fruit: Dense, starchy, white to cream colored

varieties.name: OTEA
varieties.traits: Round, firm fruit, similar appearance to Hawaiian variety
varieties.flesh_fruit: Dense, starchy texture, excellent eating quality

varieties.name: MAOPO
varieties.traits: Large, round fruit. Tall-growing variety (preferred for timber)
varieties.flesh_fruit: Light texture, similar to Ma'afala

varieties.name: PUOU
varieties.traits: Distinctive less-segmented leaves compared to other varieties
varieties.flesh_fruit: Good quality, versatile for cooking

varieties.name: CANNONBALL
varieties.traits: Round fruit, dense, named for cannonball-like weight
varieties.flesh_fruit: Firm, dense texture

varieties.name: AFRICAN BREADFRUIT (Treculia africana)
varieties.traits: Seeds ground into breadfruit flour. Much hardier than true breadfruit
varieties.availability: Fruitopia Nursery (Brisbane/Queensland), grown accustomed to Queensland weather

varieties.name: KADACHAKKA (KERALA LOCAL)
varieties.traits: Medium to large fruit, green skin, commonly available in Kerala markets
varieties.flesh_fruit: White to cream, excellent for curries (Kadachakka Mappas, Kadachakka Masala)
varieties.yield_notes: Good production, fruits 600-2000 per mature tree annually
varieties.availability: Local Kerala nurseries, agricultural stations, farmer-to-farmer exchange (root suckers)

varieties.name: MA'AFALA (SAMOAN)
varieties.traits: Dwarf variety (3m fruiting height), small-medium fruit (0.8-1.6kg)
varieties.flesh_fruit: Creamy white turning yellow when cooked, light fluffy texture, highest protein (6%)
varieties.availability: Can be imported via Breadfruit Institute network, tissue culture labs

varieties.name: HAWAIIAN 'ULU MAOLI
varieties.traits: Medium fruit (1.6kg average), firm, dense
varieties.flesh_fruit: Dense, starchy, excellent for frying, chips

varieties.name: OTEA (TAHITIAN)
varieties.traits: Round, firm fruit
varieties.flesh_fruit: Dense, starchy, excellent quality

varieties.name: ULU FITI (FIJIAN)
varieties.traits: Medium-large fruit with edible seeds
varieties.flesh_fruit: Tender, moist, sweet when ripe. Seeds like chestnuts when cooked

varieties.name: MAOPO (SAMOAN/TONGAN)
varieties.traits: Large, round fruit, tall-growing tree
varieties.flesh_fruit: Light texture

varieties.name: LIPET (MICRONESIAN)
varieties.traits: Very large, spiky fruit (up to 5 kg, largest variety), distinctive rough skin
varieties.flesh_fruit: Extremely light, fluffy texture

varieties.name: MEIN PADAHK (MICRONESIAN)
varieties.traits: Rough-textured skin, hybrid vigor
varieties.flesh_fruit: Not as dense as Polynesian varieties, good quality

varieties.name: PUOU
varieties.traits: Distinctive less-segmented leaves
varieties.flesh_fruit: Good quality

varieties.name: MAOHI
varieties.origin: Tahiti (French Polynesia) - genetically represents major Eastern Polynesian lineage
varieties.traits: Most common Tahitian variety, round fruit, smaller than some varieties but prolific
varieties.flesh_fruit: Smooth texture, good flavor, cooks slowly
varieties.yield_notes: Large quantity of fruit despite smaller individual size

varieties.name: ARAVEI
varieties.traits: Large fruit (20-31cm long), yellow-green skin, spiky skin when immature (spikes drop as fruit ripens)
varieties.flesh_fruit: Yellow pulp, considered among best flavors, quick-cooking

varieties.name: HAVANA
varieties.traits: Sweet, highly desirable flavor, but perishable (2-3 day shelf life after picking)
varieties.flesh_fruit: Sweet, quick-cooking, considered among most desirable

varieties.name: PAEA
varieties.traits: Large fruit (up to 28cm long)
varieties.flesh_fruit: Bright yellow pulp, flakes when cooked, good flavor, requires ~1 hour cooking over even heat

varieties.name: PUCRO
varieties.traits: Highly regarded, rough-textured yellow-green skin
varieties.flesh_fruit: Smooth yellow pulp, cooks quickly, one of finest flavors

varieties.name: AFARA
varieties.traits: UNUSUAL - only variety with pinkish or orange-brown skin when mature (all others are green/yellow)
varieties.flesh_fruit: Good quality

varieties.name: AVELOLOA TALA
varieties.traits: LARGE-FRUITED variety, tall-growing tree
varieties.flesh_fruit: Good quality

varieties.name: YELLOW HEART (CARIBBEAN)
varieties.origin: Caribbean (Jamaica, Trinidad, Tobago) - descended from Capt. Bligh's 1793 Tahitian introduction
varieties.traits: Favorite variety of Jamaica, relatively high water content
varieties.flesh_fruit: Distinctive YELLOW flesh (benchmark for comparison), sweet flavor, good mouthfeel
varieties.availability: Caribbean networks, Trees That Feed Foundation

varieties.name: WHITE HEART (CARIBBEAN)
varieties.traits: Along with Yellow Heart, one of two main Caribbean varieties
varieties.flesh_fruit: White to cream colored, neutral starchy flavor (like potato), fluffy when roasted. Preferred for savory dishes where you want to control sweetness

varieties.name: CAPTAIN BLIGH
varieties.origin: Caribbean, named after the sea captain who introduced breadfruit in 1793
varieties.flesh_fruit: Good eating quality
varieties.availability: St. Vincent Botanical Gardens, Caribbean networks

varieties.name: BREADNUT (Artocarpus camansi)
varieties.origin: Native to New Guinea, Maluku Islands, Philippines
varieties.traits: Spinier fruit than cultivated breadfruit, numerous seeds
varieties.yield_notes: Good seed production, naturally propagates by seed
